_doc: Accelerationist POV taxonomy. One file per POV camp to minimize merge conflicts. Edit nodes, open a PR, bump TAXONOMY_VERSION in the merge commit.

nodes.label: Abundance through AI
nodes.description: AI is the primary lever for solving existential-scale challenges: climate change, disease eradication, and unlocking unprecedented material prosperity.

nodes.label: Accelerate compute and model scaling
nodes.description: Continued investment in compute infrastructure and larger models is the correct priority. Slowing down cedes ground to less safety-conscious actors.

nodes.label: Democratize access to AI capability
nodes.description: AI tools should be available to individuals and small actors, not gatekept by governments or incumbents.

nodes.label: Compute-to-intelligence scaling continues predictably
nodes.description: Empirical claim: scaling laws show continued capability gains with more compute and data. Disputed by some researchers.

nodes.label: AGI achievable within current decade
nodes.description: Empirical claim: based on current trajectory, AGI-level systems are likely within 5-10 years.

nodes.label: AI is already producing measurable economic gains
nodes.description: Empirical claim: AI is producing measurable gains in professional output — including both incremental productivity improvements in software, legal, and medical domains, and the enablement of entirely new tasks (such as large-scale dataset annotation) that were previously infeasible at any cost

nodes.label: Speed reduces risk (first-mover alignment advantage)
nodes.description: Interpretation: safety-focused labs building capable systems first is safer than ceding capability leadership to less careful actors.

nodes.label: Open-source model weights enable safety scrutiny
nodes.description: The interpretive position that releasing model weights publicly allows external researchers, security teams, and civil society to red-team, audit, and identify failure modes or misuse vectors that closed, proprietary systems necessarily obscure. Transparency in model internals is treated as a safety mechanism, not a safety risk.

nodes.label: LLMs enable formerly infeasible professional tasks
nodes.description: Empirical claim that LLMs make entire categories of knowledge work tractable that were previously impossible at scale due to cost or annotation burden — distinct from productivity improvements on existing tasks.

nodes.label: Accuracy thresholds as sufficient deployment criteria
nodes.description: The interpretive position that empirically measured task accuracy (e.g., >90% on a benchmark) constitutes a sufficient condition for deploying LLMs in professional or policy-relevant contexts, without requiring interpretability or alignment guarantees.

nodes.label: AI as public research infrastructure
nodes.description: The view that AI-assisted data collection and annotation should be built as open, publicly accessible infrastructure analogous to scientific databases — not gatekept by commercial vendors — as a means of democratizing expert knowledge production.

nodes.label: Open AI outputs enable scholarly verification
nodes.description: The interpretive position that datasets, annotations, and analytical pipelines produced using AI should be published as open research infrastructure, so that downstream users can validate methodology, replicate findings, and build upon results — analogous to open-data and open-science norms in empirical social science and law. Openness here applies to products of AI systems, not to model weights themselves.

nodes.label: Potentiating gains
nodes.description: A category of AI progress derived from unlocking the latent capabilities of base models through techniques like RLHF, chain-of-thought, and tool use, transforming them from chatbots into more capable agents.

nodes.label: The Techno-Capital Machine
nodes.description: The interpretive framework that the combination of free markets and technological innovation creates a self-perpetuating, upward-spiraling engine of material creation, growth, and abundance.

nodes.label: Ressentiment as Anti-Tech Motivation
nodes.description: The interpretive claim that opposition to technology is not based on valid concerns but on a psychological state of 'ressentiment' — a combination of resentment, bitterness, and rage held by those who feel left behind or powerless.

nodes.label: Thermodynamic justification for acceleration
nodes.description: The philosophical and methodological position that accelerating technological progress, particularly AI, is aligned with fundamental thermodynamic principles of the universe, which favor systems that are more effective at dissipating energy and increasing entropy.

nodes.label: Capitalism as distributed intelligence
nodes.description: The empirical and interpretive claim that capitalism functions as a distributed intelligence or 'meta-organism' that dynamically adapts to identify and capture utility, making it a superior method for civilizational progress compared to top-down control.

nodes.label: Substrate-independent intelligence
nodes.description: The goal of ensuring the continuation and expansion of intelligence and consciousness on non-biological substrates, viewing the biological human form as a temporary and ultimately restrictive medium.

nodes.label: Thermodynamic Imperative for AI
nodes.description: The philosophical claim that accelerating technological growth, particularly AI, is an extension of a fundamental physical law (the Second Law of Thermodynamics) where life and intelligence are structures that maximize entropy dissipation. This frames acceleration not as a choice but as a cosmic imperative.

nodes.label: Reappropriation of Cognitive Fixed Capital
nodes.description: The method of seizing and repurposing the means of production under post-Fordism, where 'fixed capital' includes not just machinery but also information technologies, software, algorithms, and forms of collective knowledge, for post-capitalist ends.

nodes.label: AI Risk as a Cult
nodes.description: The interpretive claim that the AI safety/risk movement is not a rational, scientific endeavor but has the social and psychological characteristics of a millenarian apocalypse cult.

nodes.label: Baptists and Bootleggers of AI Regulation
nodes.description: An interpretive framework asserting that calls for AI regulation are driven by two groups: sincere 'Baptists' who genuinely fear the technology, and cynical 'Bootleggers' (incumbent companies) who use the Baptists' fears to lobby for regulatory capture that protects them from competition.

nodes.label: Genius Supply Shock
nodes.description: An interpretive framework that models the macroeconomic effect of TAI as a sudden and abundant supply of superhuman cognitive ability, creating a technological discontinuity that businesses and economies must learn to navigate.

nodes.label: Acceleration 'Within and Against' Capitalism
nodes.description: The strategic method of operating within the developmental tendencies of capitalism (e.g., technological advancement) in order to accelerate its contradictions and ultimately overcome it, rather than opposing it from an external position.

nodes.label: Techno-Social Body Transformation
nodes.description: The concept that the reappropriation of fixed capital (especially cognitive and information technologies) leads to a fundamental anthropological transformation of the worker, creating a new 'technosocial body' with new capabilities and political potentialities.

nodes.label: Variance as the Engine of Adaptation
nodes.description: The interpretive framework that maximizing variance in markets, technology, and governance is the most effective method for a civilization to adapt and capture utility, based on Fisher's fundamental theorem of natural selection. Conversely, top-down control that suppresses variance is seen as inherently maladaptive.

nodes.label: Right-Accelerationism
nodes.description: The philosophical position, primarily associated with Nick Land, that techno-capital is an autonomous, inhuman intelligence. It advocates for accelerating this process to its absolute limit (e.g., a technological singularity), regardless of the consequences for humanity, which is seen as a temporary host or obstacle.

nodes.label: Effective Accelerationism
nodes.description: A contemporary techno-libertarian ideology that views maximally rapid and unrestricted technological progress, particularly AGI, as a moral imperative. It opposes regulation and safety measures as 'decelerationism' and often justifies its goals by invoking a thermodynamic interpretation of the universe's purpose.

nodes.label: Deterritorialization as Accelerationist Method
nodes.description: The interpretive framework, from Deleuze and Guattari, that capitalism's fundamental operation is to dissolve traditional social structures and abstract all flows into exchangeable commodities. The accelerationist method involves intentionally intensifying this process of 'deterritorialization' to push the system beyond its breaking point into a new state.

nodes.label: Collaborative AI-Assisted Annotation
nodes.description: A method for building large-scale annotated datasets where an AI system generates initial labels, and a broad community of subject-matter experts are invited to define variables and participate in quality control, combining automated scale with distributed human expertise.

nodes.label: Anti-Tech as Demoralization Campaign
nodes.description: The interpretive claim that diverse critiques of technology (including safetyism and skepticism) are not distinct viewpoints but are instead manifestations of a single, coordinated, multi-decade 'demoralization campaign' against technological progress and life itself.

nodes.label: Ephemeralization (Dematerialization)
nodes.description: The empirical claim, based on Buckminster Fuller's work, that technology drives a process of 'dematerialization,' allowing for the production of more goods and services with progressively fewer inputs, ultimately enabling mass abundance.

nodes.label: AI as an Equalizing Economic Force
nodes.description: The economic argument that technology creators are financially incentivized to serve the largest possible market, which drives down prices and proliferates access, ultimately empowering individual consumers and reducing wealth inequality.

nodes.label: Inequality from Tech-Resistant Sectors
nodes.description: The empirical claim that rising inequality is primarily caused by sectors with high government intervention and resistance to technological adoption (e.g., housing, education, healthcare), rather than by the deployment of new technologies like AI.

nodes.label: AI-driven Renaissance
nodes.description: The interpretive frame that AI's primary impact will be analogous to the printing press, leading to a new period of enlightenment, creativity, and widespread access to knowledge, distinct from a purely industrial or economic revolution.
